content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

Rourke & Woodruff
DAVID A. DE BERRY
CRAIG G. FARRINGTON
JOSEPH W. FORBATH
LOIS E. JEFFREY
DANIEL E. LUNDY
CHERYL LYNN
RAYENE MAZAREI
BETTY C. MRGUDICH
THOMAS F. NIXON
JAMES G. ROURKE
JOHN R. SHAW
KENNARD R. SMART, JR.
DANIEL SPENCE
WILLIAM C. TAYLOR
DAVID T. WARD
ALAN R. WATTS
WAYNE W. WINTHERS
THOMAS L. WOODRUFF

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SUITE 7000

TELEPHONE (714) 558-7000
FACSIMILE (714) 835-7787

701 SOUTH PARKER STREET
ORANGE, CALIFORNIA 92666

(714) 564-2608

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

April 16, 1992

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

Our firm represents the Orange County Transportation Authority
(OCTA) and its affiliated agencies as the General Counsel. The
OCTA affiliated agencies are separate legal entities governed by
the same Board of Directors that governs the OCTA.

This letter is a request for formal written advice (2 Cal.
Code of Regs. section 18329(b)). This request is made on behalf of
the Board of Directors and alternate Directors of the OCTA. The
names and mailing addresses of the Directors and alternates are
attached. We are the authorized representative of the Board
members and alternates for purposes of this request.

The facts material to the consideration of the question
presented are provided in this letter.

OCTA is subject to the provisions of Section 84308 and we have
a question regarding the point in time when the prohibition
provisions of Section 84308 commence.

OCTA receives many proposals from potential contractors to
provide OCTA with personalty or services. The proposals are
usually submitted in response to an OCTA Request for Proposal
(RFP). Often many proposals are received in response to a single
RFP. Generally proposals are received by OCTA staff approximately
two months prior to the Board of Directors selecting one of the
proposals. Staff evaluates the proposals and narrows the field to
the most qualified proposals (usually three). Information
regarding these finalist proposals is presented to an OCTA

Page 2                                                April 16, 1992


committee composed of Directors and alternates (less than a
majority of the Board of Directors) for a committee recommendation
to the full Board of Directors. Information regarding proposals
not included in the finalist proposals is not presented to the
Board of Directors or the Board Committee. The information is
presented to the Board Committee approximately ten days prior to
the Board meeting.  A staff report describing the finalist
proposals and the committee recommendation is included in the
agenda materials distributed to the members of the Board of
Directors approximately six days prior to the Board of Directors
meeting when the Board is scheduled to select one of the finalist
proposals.

     Language in a previous FPPC Advice Letter (Advice Letter to
Gregory Thatch, May 15, 1989, No. I-89-222, at p. 4) suggests that
OCTA members may not solicit or accept more than $250 from a party
which has merely submitted a proposal to OCTA in response to an RFP
after the proposal has been submitted.  It appears this conclusion
contradicts Sections 18438.2 and 18438.7 of Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, which apply to Section 84308.  Regulation
18438.2(b)(2) provides that a proceeding is not pending before an
agency for purposes of Section 84308 unless the Directors are
required by law to make a decision or the matter has been otherwise
submitted to the Board of Directors for its decision.  Regulation
18438.7(b) provides that a Director knows, or should have known,
about a proceeding pending before the OCTA Board of Directors if
the Director has actual knowledge or has received notice of the
proceeding.  "Notice includes receipt of an agenda or docket
identifying the proceeding and the party or other persons affected
by name." (Regulation 18438.7(b)(1))

     We believe the contradiction is presented by the Thatch Advice
Letter holding that a Board member is prohibited from soliciting or
accepting a campaign contribution in excess of $250 from a party
which has submitted a proposal, even though the Board member at
that time has no actual knowledge and has received no notice of
either the pendency of the proceeding or the submittal of a
proposal by the party.

     The procurement process at OCTA is significantly different
from the process described at SETA in the Thatch letter. According
to the letter, the SETA governing board may still review a proposal
even after the staff deems it nonresponsive; according to OCTA
procedures, the Board of Directors does not have any involvement or
knowledge of proposals that the staff does not place on its
recommendation list.  In short, the staff at SETA apparently only
reviews proposals to see whether they meet technical guidelines,
whereas OCTA staff actually evaluates proposals and chooses the top
contenders.  We believe these differences in the procurement
process support the conclusion that OCTA proceedings do not

Page 3                                                April 16, 1992


commence until the Board members and alternates receive the agenda
materials, or actual notice, whichever occurs earlier.

     Compliance with the Thatch Advice Letter presents a near
impossible situation.  The OCTA Board of Directors has eleven
members and three alternates.  OCTA annually has several hundred
procurements which are subject to the provisions of Section 84308.
Each procurement often is pursued by several separate parties and
each party often includes several independent subcontractors.  If
receipt by staff of a proposal triggers the prohibition provisions
of Section 84308, then each OCTA Board member and alternate will
have no actual knowledge and will have received no notice and will
have no means of identifying the party(s) which the member or
alternate is prohibited from soliciting or accepting a campaign
contribution of more than $250.  In order to assure compliance with
this interpretation of the law, the members and alternates are
effectively prohibited from soliciting or accepting any campaign
contributions of more than $250 from anyone.  Certainly this is not
the intent or purpose of Section 84308.

     In contrast, if the point in time when staff initially informs
a Board member, either through the committee process or the Board
agenda materials, about the proposals the Board is being asked to
consider, is the trigger point, then the Board members will have
actual knowledge or will have received notice of the parties to a
pending OCTA proceeding.  The prohibition provisions of Section
84308 will then apply to those parties.  This is the interpretation
that is consistent with the Regulations, and the intent and purpose
of Section 84308.

     Thank you very much for your attention to this issue that is
important to the OCTA and its affiliated agencies.

                                             Sincerely,

                                             ROURKE & WOODRUFF

                                             [signature]
                                             KENNARD R. SMART, JR.

KRS:lw:R:4/7/92(D46)

cc:  Members and Alternates
     of the OCTA Board of Directors
     Mr. Stan Oftelie, C.E.O., OCTA

ORANGE COUNTY TRANSPORTATION AUTHORITY
AND AFFILIATED AGENCIES


Board of Directors and Alternates

Roger R. Stanton, Chairman

Irv Pickler, Vice Chairman

Gary L. Hausdorfer, Director

William D. Mahoney, Director

Dana Reed, Director

Thomas F. Riley, Director

Don R. Roth, Director

Charles V. Smith, Director

Bob Wahlstrom, Director

Harriett M. Wieder, Director

Daniel H. Young, Director

Sarah L. Catz, Alternate

Eileen Krause, Alternate

Gaddi Vasquez, Alternate


ADDRESS FOR DIRECTORS AND ALTERNATES:

Orange County Transportation Authority
1055 North Main Street
Suite 516
Santa Ana, CA  92701

sections.question_synthetic: At what point in time does Section 84308's prohibition on soliciting or accepting contributions commence for OCTA Board members—when staff receives a proposal or when Board members receive actual notice through committee information or agenda materials?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. The letter contains the question implicit in the body text but no formal Q&A sections. OCTA is requesting formal written advice per California Code of Regulations section 18329(b). The letter references a prior FPPC Advice Letter (Thatch, 1989, No. I-89-222) and disputes its interpretation.

embedding.summary: The Orange County Transportation Authority (OCTA), through its General Counsel, requests formal FPPC advice regarding the timing of when Section 84308 contribution restrictions apply to Board members in the context of OCTA's procurement process, challenging an earlier FPPC advice letter that the requester believes creates practical compliance difficulties.
